sections.title: Can Do: -(으)ㄹ 수 있다
sections.explanation: The most common way to express ability or possibility in Korean is -(으)ㄹ 수 있다. You take the verb stem, add -(으)ㄹ (the future/prospective modifier), then 수 (a bound noun meaning 'way/method'), then 있다 (to exist). Literally it means 'there exists a way to [verb].' Add -ㄹ if the stem ends in a vowel, -을 if it ends in a consonant. Examples: 가다 → 갈 수 있다 (can go). 먹다 → 먹을 수 있다 (can eat). 만들다 → 만들 수 있다 (can make — ㄹ stems keep their ㄹ). 하다 → 할 수 있다 (can do). In polite speech: 갈 수 있어요, 먹을 수 있어요. This pattern expresses both learned ability ('I can swim') and situational possibility ('I can go tomorrow').
sections.tip: 수 is always separated by a space from the modifier and from 있다: 갈 수 있다 (three-word structure). Never write 갈수있다 without spaces.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I can read Korean'?
exercises.explanation: 읽다 → stem 읽 (ends in consonant) + 을 수 있다 → 읽을 수 있어요. Always use the -(으)ㄹ modifier form before 수.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form for 만들다 (to make) + -(으)ㄹ 수 있다?
exercises.explanation: 만들다 has a ㄹ stem. ㄹ stems already end in ㄹ, so you just add 수 있다 directly: 만들 수 있다.
만나다 → 만날 수 있다 means 'can meet.' The question form 만날 수 있어요? asks about possibility: 'Can we meet today?'

sections.title: Cannot Do: -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 and 못
sections.explanation: There are two main ways to express inability in Korean. First, -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 — the negative of -(으)ㄹ 수 있다. Simply replace 있다 with 없다: 갈 수 없다 (cannot go), 먹을 수 없다 (cannot eat). Second, the short negation 못 placed before the verb: 못 가다 (cannot go), 못 먹다 (cannot eat), 못 하다 (cannot do). For 하다 compound verbs, 못 goes between the noun and 하다: 운동 못 하다 (cannot exercise), or you can say 운동을 못 하다. Both forms express inability, but 못 is more common in casual speech. 못 specifically implies inability (not lack of permission). For lack of permission, use -(으)면 안 되다 instead.
sections.tip: 못 is short and punchy for conversation. -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 is slightly more formal or emphatic. Both are correct and interchangeable for expressing inability. But 못 cannot be used with adjectives — only with action verbs.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct short negation for 운동하다 (to exercise)?
exercises.explanation: For 하다 compound verbs, 못 goes between the noun and 하다 (conjugated): 운동 못 해요. Alternatively: 운동을 못 해요.
못 before a verb expresses inability. 노래를 못 해요 = I can't sing (I lack the ability). 'I don't sing' (by choice) would be 노래를 안 해요.

exercises.prompt: Why can't you say '못 예뻐요' to mean 'can't be pretty'?
options: 못 cannot be used with adjectives
못 can only be used in past tense
exercises.explanation: 못 is only used with action verbs to express inability to perform an action. It cannot be used with descriptive verbs (adjectives) like 예쁘다, 크다, 좋다.

sections.title: Strong Inability: -지 못하다
sections.explanation: A third way to express inability is the long negation pattern: verb stem + -지 못하다. This attaches -지 to the verb stem, then adds 못하다. Examples: 가다 → 가지 못하다 (cannot go). 먹다 → 먹지 못하다 (cannot eat). 이해하다 → 이해하지 못하다 (cannot understand). This form works with any verb, including 하다 compounds, without word-order concerns. It often carries a nuance of 'failed to do' or 'was unable to despite wanting to.' In polite speech: 가지 못해요, 먹지 못했어요. This pattern is especially common in written Korean and formal speech. Compare: 못 갔어요 (casual: couldn't go) vs. 가지 못했어요 (formal/literary: was unable to go).
sections.tip: Use -지 못하다 when you want to emphasize that the inability was unfortunate or that you tried but failed. It also avoids the word-order issues of 못 with 하다 compounds — 공부하지 못하다 is always clear.

examples.en: It rained, so I couldn't go
I'm sorry I couldn't keep the promise
I didn't have time, so I couldn't finish

exercises.prompt: How do you express 'I couldn't sleep' using -지 못하다?
exercises.explanation: 자다 (to sleep) → stem 자 + -지 못하다 → 자지 못하다. Past tense: 자지 못했어요.

exercises.prompt: Which nuance does -지 못하다 often carry compared to 못?
options: Tried but was unable / regret about inability
exercises.explanation: -지 못하다 often implies that you wanted to do something but were unable, or that you regret the inability. It sounds more formal and sometimes more empathetic than the short 못.

options: I'm sorry I didn't want to attend
exercises.explanation: 참석하다 (to attend) + -지 못하다 = couldn't attend. The sentence politely apologizes for being unable to attend.
